title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Culinary Compass: Food as Cultural Language
content.subtitle: How what we eat defines our social boundaries and collective identity.
content.text: Food is rarely just about sustenance; it is a complex semiotic system, a language that communicates who we are, where we come from, and who we exclude. Anthropologists have long noted that the act of 'breaking bread' is the foundational ritual of human society. [31] However, the rules governing what is considered 'edible' are not dictated by biology, but by a dense web of cultural taboos and historical accidents. [/31] What is a delicacy in one corner of the globe may be viewed with visceral disgust in another, proving that the palate is trained by the community rather than the stomach. || [32] This 'cultural flavoring' of our biology serves an evolutionary purpose: it acts as a social glue, reinforcing the boundaries of the 'in-group.' [/32] By sharing specific meals prepared in specific ways, a community reaffirms its shared history. This is particularly evident in diaspora communities, where traditional recipes are preserved with a religious fervor. For those living far from their ancestral lands, a specific spice or cooking technique becomes a portable homeland, a way to resist the homogenizing forces of their new environment. || [33] Interestingly, the rise of 'foodie' culture in the West has transformed this internal social glue into a form of external 'cultural capital.' [/33] Knowledge of authentic, obscure cuisines is no longer just about heritage; it is a marker of status and global sophistication. We 'consume' other cultures to demonstrate our own cosmopolitanism. Yet, this often leads to 'culinary tourism,' where a dish is stripped of its historical context and reduced to an aesthetic experience for the affluent. The sacred becomes a commodity. || Furthermore, the industrialization of food has created a 'nutritional amnesia.' [34] In many developed nations, the link between the soil and the plate has been so thoroughly severed that consumers no longer recognize the raw ingredients of their meals. [/34] This detachment has profound psychological consequences, leading to a loss of the communal 'commensality'—the shared experience of the meal—that once anchored human life. We eat more, but we dine less, often consuming calories in isolation while staring at screens. || [35] Some activists are attempting to bridge this gap through the 'Slow Food' movement, which advocates for a return to local, seasonal eating and a celebration of regional diversity. [/35] While noble, the movement faces the criticism of being inherently elitist. After all, the 'luxury' of spending hours preparing a meal from scratch is only available to those with the gift of time and financial security. For the working poor, the efficiency of processed food is not a choice, but a necessity. || [36] Ultimately, the future of food will be a battle between the convenience of global monoculture and the resilience of local tradition. [/36] As climate change and resource scarcity force us to rethink our diets, the challenge will be to create a food system that is sustainable without being soul-destroying. We must find a way to feed the world's population while preserving the 'flavor' of the human experience, remembering that when we lose a recipe, we lose a piece of our collective memory.

questions.question: What is the author's main point about 'edibility' in the first paragraph?
options: A) It is a biological instinct that humans are born with.
B) It has remained consistent across all human civilizations.
C) It is primarily determined by the nutritional value of the food.
D) It is defined by social norms rather than physical needs.
questions.explanation: The text states that rules of what is edible are 'not dictated by biology, but by a dense web of cultural taboos.'

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, why do diaspora communities hold onto traditional recipes?
options: A) Because they are required to do so by their religious beliefs.
B) Because they are unable to find modern ingredients in their new homes.
C) To prove that their ancestral cooking is superior to local food.
D) To maintain a sense of identity and connection to their origins.
questions.explanation: The author describes traditional food as a 'portable homeland' and a way to 'reaffirm shared history' and resist environmental homogenization.

questions.question: The author uses the term 'cultural capital' to describe...
options: A) the investment needed to open an authentic ethnic restaurant.
B) the money generated by the global food industry.
C) the use of food knowledge to show social status and worldly experience.
D) the value of traditional recipes in their original context.
questions.explanation: The text says knowledge of obscure cuisines is a 'marker of status' used to 'demonstrate our own cosmopolitanism.'

questions.question: What is meant by 'nutritional amnesia' in the fourth paragraph?
options: A) The inability to remember the flavors of home-cooked meals.
B) Forgetting to eat healthy meals due to a busy schedule.
C) The loss of knowledge regarding where our food comes from.
D) A medical condition caused by consuming too many processed calories.
questions.explanation: The text explains this as the link between 'the soil and the plate' being severed, so consumers don't recognize raw ingredients.

questions.question: What is the main criticism of the 'Slow Food' movement mentioned in the text?
options: A) It ignores the environmental benefits of commercial farming.
B) It is not effective at improving the quality of local ingredients.
C) it encourages the consumption of unhealthy seasonal fats.
D) It is only accessible to people with certain economic advantages.
questions.explanation: The author calls it 'inherently elitist' because it requires 'the gift of time and financial security' which the working poor lack.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author suggests that the biggest challenge for the future is...
options: A) convincing people to prioritize taste over convenience.
B) finding enough food to feed the entire world population.
C) balancing environmental sustainability with cultural preservation.
D) replacing all local traditions with a more efficient global monoculture.
questions.explanation: The text states the challenge is to create a system that is 'sustainable without being soul-destroying' (preserving the 'flavor' of human experience).
